concept.pre:
Vi has many commands which are formed by the letter followed by a
movement command. For instance, the "w" command moves the cursor
to the next word. The "dw" command deletes to the next word. If the
command is preceded by a number, it is as if the movement command was
preceded by that number. "45dw" is the same as "d45w". If the command
is just repeated ("dd"), it applies the operation to the current line.
If V is a vi command that is supposed to be followed by a movement command,
what does "VV" do?
Execute the "V" command to the current line Execute the "V" command twice Execute the "V" command once (Ignoring errors) If V is a vi command that is supposed to be followed by a movement command,
what does "25Vb" do?
Applies the "V" command to previous 25 words Applies the "V" command 25 times to the previous word Applies the "Vb" command to line 25 If V is a vi command that is supposed to be followed by a movement command,
what does "3Vj" do?
The same thing as "V3j" The same thing as "Vj3" The same thing as "Vjjj"

The "c" command (change) is followed by a movement command. It replaces
the text between the cursor and the specified position with text terminated
by an escape key.
What does "c3wfred the farmer<escape>" do?
Change the next three words to "fred the farmer". Change the next word with "fred the farmerfred the farmerfred the farmer". Change the next three words to "fred the farmer<escape>". What does "3ccralph<escape>? do?
Change the next three lines to the word "ralph" Change the current line three times to "ralph" Change line three to "ralph"

The ">" command is followed by a movement command. It shifts text one
shift width to the right. "<" shifts text one shift width to the left.
(To set your shift width to four, type ":set sw=4<return>") Shifting only
works on a line by line basis (I.E. <w" is illegal).
What does ">>" do?
Shift the current line right one shift width Shift the current line right two shift widths Put the cursor at the end of the line What does "4>" do?
Nothing, the shift command requires a movement command. Shift four lines to the right Shift four characters to the right What does ">4w" do?
Nothing. (Illegal command) Shifting can only be done to lines Shift four words to the right How do you set your shift width to 7?
":set sw=7<return>" ":set sw=7" "set sw=7<return>" "set sw 7<return>" "set sw 7"

The "~" command changes the case of the current character and moves the
cursor to the next character. (I.E. if the cursor is on an "A", it replaces
it with "a", or if the cursor is on a "b", it replaces it with "B".) "~"
does nothing to non-alphabetic characters. Unfortunately, preceding the
"~" command with a number does nothing useful.
What command changes the case of the next character?
~ c cl cc

Typing "vi" runs the UNIX editor "ex" in visual mode (makes use of
smart or semi smart terminals). Typing "ex" runs the UNIX editor
in non-visual mode (dumb mode). Although visual mode provides most of
the normal editing functions, it lacks substitution and extended file
handling capabilities. To execute a non-visual ex command from visual
mode, precede the command with a colon (:).
How do you execute a non-visual command from visual mode in ex?
Precede the command with a colon (:). Precede the command with a "Q".

Non-visual commands are usually designed to work on a line by line basis.
A line specification is called an "address". Most commands can be applied
to a range of lines. A range of lines is specified by an address followed
by a comma (,) followed by another address. For instance, to apply the
command "fred" to lines 45 to 70, one might type ":45,70fred<return>". You
can use "." in place of a number to indicate the current line. ":.,20fred"
will apply the command "fred" to the current line through line 70. You
can use "$" in place of a number to indicate the last line. ".,$fred<return>"
means execute the "fred" command on everthing from here to end of file.
Also, you may use limited math in your address specification. For instance,
".+23" means "23 lines from here". ":45,.+40fred<return>" would apply the
command to lines 45 through the current line number plus 40.
How do you apply the non-visual command V to lines 20 through the line
after the current line?
:20,.+1V<return> :20G,jV<return> :20-.+1V<return> How could you "fred" every line in the file (in non-visual mode)?
:1,$fred<return> :fred<return> :1,Gfred<return> The command ":5,.V<return>":
Applies the command V to lines 5 through the current line Executes the colon command from line 5 to the current line followed by the V command

The non-visual command "d" deletes a line. ":10,20d<return>" would delete
lines 10 through 20.
